title: Scrubbing a secret in the working tree doesn't remove it from git history
rule: Making a private repository public exposes the entire commit history, not just the current files. Removing or hashing a sensitive value in the working tree does NOT purge it from past commits — anyone can recover it with git log -p. Before flipping a repo public, scan the full history for secrets and sensitive identifiers; if any are found, rewrite history first (squash to a fresh initial commit, or use git filter-repo) and force-push. Treat the flip as irreversible: forks, archivers, and scrapers can copy anything during the exposure window, so do the history hygiene before, not after.
triggers: making a private repo public exposes git history
git log -p still shows a deleted secret
remove a secret from git history before going public
squash or filter-repo to purge history before public flip
